data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street on Friday snapped a two-week losing streak, with the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ending just -0.07% below its record closing high.

It was a week dominated by U.S. President Donald Trump's announcements on tariffs. On Monday, he announced 25% levies on all steel and aluminum imports into the U.S. On Thursday, he signed a memorandum that tasked his administration with investigating current trade relations and proposing reciprocal tariffs on a country-specific basis.

Still, the fact that the president did not immediately put into effect the tit-for-tat tariffs and was scant on details helped market sentiment.

A favorable producer inflation report for January on Thursday also helped, as the components in it that feed directly into the Federal Reserve's favorite inflation gauge came in soft. It also helped overcome the shock provided by a hotter-than-expected consumer inflation report on Wednesday.

For the week, the S&P 500 (SP500) gained +1.5%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) surged +2.6%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) added +0.5%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

data.title: Fannie Mae: Checklist For Privatization
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

The Federal National Mortgage Association, also known as Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA), is currently one of the most talked-about opportunities among retail investors, due to its potential upside. Billionaire hedge fund manager Bill Ackman is a big fan of the stock and argued in a January 16th presentation that shares have a lot of upside in the event of a spin out.

I believe one of the main keys right now is not if the Trump admin wants to privatize, but when. I think it’s quite likely that this will happen, so the main question surrounding the performance of the stock right now is how it will happen.

The administration has signaled that any privatization will need to answer a key question: will privatization keep mortgage rates low? For investors the capital stack question remains: will the senior preferred debt be wiped from the company’s balance sheet? On both of these questions I remain cautiously optimistic.

For now, I am a strong buy (much like how I am on the sister company Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC), but I see these as two main questions remaining.

Background

Last month, I wrote an article about Fannie Mae’s sister company, the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation, also known as Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC). In this article, I outlined a lot of the reasons why I was incredibly optimistic about both sister companies.

First of all, it’s important to note that both companies — and therefore, both stocks — more or less move together due to any privatization affecting both or none of them.

During that last piece of research on Freddie Mac, I described how Ackman believes the stocks could reach the $31-$34 range each if they are privatized. Given this would obviously apply to both companies, it would mean a major jump in shares, given that at the time of this writing, shares are worth $6.72 ((OTCQB:FNMA)) and $6.12 (OTCQB:FMCC) respectively.

As of right now, a month after my last writing, the story is similar, but we’ve gathered key from the Trump administration on where things could head.

With this, my point here is to show the reasonable bull case with the risks of the senior preferred shares and the desire from Treasury Secretary Bessent to keep mortgage rates low.

Where Do We Stand?

One of the biggest things that has happened in the effort to get Fannie Mae privatized is Housing Urban Secretary (HUD) Scott Turner’s push. Turner, a former NFL player, was confirmed on February 5th by the U.S. Senate to be the new head of the Department of Urban Housing and Development.

Turner announced last Thursday, February 6th, just one day after taking over his new role, that he will “quarterback” the efforts in getting Fannie Mae privatized and out of the control of the U.S. Government. During the 2008 recession, the U.S. Government took control of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac as the two companies were struggling with bad mortgage debt.

In a recent interview, Turner touched on the push to privatize Fannie Mae, exclaiming that “there are partners that will be at the table and obviously we’ll be one of them,” when it comes to releasing Fannie Mae from the Government’s control.

The fact that Turner made this announcement the day after assuming his new role shows me that this is something he is very serious about and plans to attack quickly. This is encouraging to me.

He will need to get other stakeholders on board. So far there looks like there’s interest.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent offered his thoughts on the plan to privatize the two companies and get them out of control of the Government. According to Bessent, any release of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac from their current conservatorship would depend on the implications for mortgage rates.

Right now, the priority is tax policy — once we get through that, we will think about [reforms to Fannie and Freddie], Bessent said after an interview with Bloomberg’s Saleha Mohsin on Thursday, February 6.

Bessent added: Anything that is done around a safe and sound release is going to hinge on the effect of long-term mortgage rates.

It’s clear that keeping mortgage rates low is one of the biggest things stopping the two companies from being privatized almost immediately. However, it’s a bit of a catch-22, because Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac help keep mortgage rates low.

According to JVM Lending, mortgage rates are much lower under Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac because “the government guarantees every loan underwritten to their standards.” In this case, both Fannie and Freddie’s borrowing costs are low because their cost of funds are low because the government backstops their borrowing efforts.

Fannie’s rates are particularly low for first-time homebuyers with small down payments (under 10%). Without Fannie and Freddie, many of those buyers would have trouble getting financing at all, JVM Lending claims.

Adding to the mortgage rate risk (which has to be answered before a privatization), there is a risk of excess dilution for common shareholders, due to a 4% capital requirement. Ackman claims this bar is too high, according to his presentation.

The current 4% capital requirement is too high and would increase the cost of mortgage financing at a time when home affordability is at record lows and mortgage rates are at multiyear highs… 4% capital would require ~$270 billion to support the single-family guarantee business alone, >2x the capital held by both entities today. This would tie up ~$100bn+ of wasted capital that could be better deployed in other US businesses.

To address this (and the over dilution issue for shareholders), Ackman believes a 2.5% equity capital requirement could allow the mortgage buyer to still charge a guarantee fee and still produce enough net income to justify its capital ratios. This could mean we can keep guarantee fees at the levels roughly where they are today.

To help keep the cost of funds low for Fannie (and Freddie) the government should forgive its senior preferred shares like Ackman suggested. The US government has made far more than its principal amount back. Dropping these shares would save billions of dollars annually in interest.

Valuation

As I mentioned earlier in this article and in my previous research, Ackman believes that shares could be worth anywhere from $31 to $34 a piece if Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac are released from the control of the Government. However, this will only be attainable if we see an IPO (Initial Public Offering) or a release from the Government into two private entities.

Because of this, I think we (like with Freddie) still need to figure out what the blended valuation of shares is (equal weighted chance we IPO and equal chance the IPO doesn’t go through). I believe that there is definitely a non-zero chance that this happens. I think this will give us a middle of the road scenario of what shares are worth.

As I wrote about last time, shares of Fannie Mae (like Freddie) are worth, essentially, nothing if the company is unable to separate into a private entity due to the Senior Preferred debt. However, I think this is unlikely to happen. I think the eventual outcome of what will happen is closer to Ackman’s belief of $31 to $34 per share, but it’s important to recognize that this outcome is a possibility.

In the bull case, I agree with Ackman’s opinion that shares will be worth roughly $31 to $34 if they can separate from the conservatorship and spin out in a capital efficient manner. I arrive at Ackman’s valuation based on the company trading at 10 times its last 12 months net income.

In 2023 (last full year of earnings) Fannie Mae brought in a net income of about $17.5 billion. If we multiply this number by 10, we arrive at a market cap of about $175 billion. Based on information provided and explained later in this article, the U.S. Government will own 79.9% of shares due to outstanding warrants.

This means that the Non-U.S. Government market cap is roughly $36.75 billion. With 1.158 billion shares currently outstanding, we arrive at value of roughly $31 per share. I think my conservative P/E multiple of 10 should account for the fact that the real forward P/E multiple will probably be closer to 14 (but also offset by the fact shareholders will be diluted in an IPO).

So because of this, I still agree with my opinion from my last article on Freddie Mac that to take the middle of the road approach, shares should trade at the sector median forward price-to-sales ratio to reflect both the bearish possibility that shares are worth nothing and the bullish outcome, in which shares are worth more than $30.

Currently, Fannie Mae’s forward price-to-sales ratio is 1.35, which is 53.54% lower than the sector median of 2.91. Seeking Alpha Quant gives Fannie Mae a grade of an A- on this metric. But like I mentioned in my last article, I think there is a lot of room for upside in shares if we saw them converge at the sector median of 2.91.

If we saw shares converge on this forward Price/sales ratio, this would represent about 115.5% upside from here.

I still agree with my previous thesis on its sister firm that shares should be priced more toward the bullish side than the bearish side. There has been a strong push from many inside the administration to get this deal done. I think they know (like many shareholders do) that the market will only accept a private Fannie Mae in the event that Senior Preferred debt is wiped off the balance sheet (otherwise the risk of dilution would be huge).

Risks

I think the big risk here is that mortgage rates do not go down in a simulated privatization, which is a big key in getting Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac privatized, as I mentioned earlier with Bessent’s quote.

To counter this, there is a bull case, and that is to simply wait.

It sounds odd, but the Treasury has a series of warrants that “give Treasury the right, though not the obligation, to buy common stock in the GSEs for a nominal price in the future.” These warrants expire on September 7, 2028.

According to this document from the Congressional Budget Office:

...if the Treasury exercised all of its warrants, it would own 79.9 percent of the GSEs’ common shares issued before the new stock offering. As a result, the Treasury would be in line to receive 79.9 percent of the equity value available to existing holders of common shares after the GSEs made up their capital shortfall and redeemed their senior and junior preferred shares.

If this September 7, 2028, deadline passes, the warrants will expire, which means a spinout would be a lot more feasible with far less dilution. While common shareholders (myself included) definitely don’t want to wait, this provides a scenario where the government could convert its senior preferred shares to common stock, while not diluting shares so much that the investment becomes unattractive. Given a late 2028 timeline, however, if the warrants expire a privatization would likely need to occur in the next presidential administration (post Trump). There is no guarantee they would support this.

Bottom Line

Fannie Mae shares are at a critical juncture. With shares seeing more than a 500% increase in the last six months, I think optimism is rising that Fannie Mae will be able to privatize and break out of their conservatorship.

This is a big opportunity, but I think the real risk now is not that Fannie Mae and its supporters don’t pursue privatization, but what cost comes with it. How we can get mortgage rates lower is key.

With this, I remain optimistic on Fannie Mae, and I continue to believe shares are a strong buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HubSpot: Despite Being Fairly Valued, The Expected Growth Is Not Enough
data.description:
HubSpot (NYSE:HUBS) is a high-quality company, being used, in the majority, by SMBs while releasing strong and differentiated gen AI features that induce personalization. I believe HubSpot is fairly valued, but the company doesn’t fill my requirements to join my portfolio, and thus I rate it a hold.

Background

HubSpot is an established CRM focused on inbound marketing. As a comprehensive CRM, HubSpot allows its customers to better manage their work around sales leads and lead development, customer contacts, marketing, personalization and content creation.

Inbound marketing is defined by the ability of the selling company to engage with an audience that finds the company’s product utility/cost very high. To do inbound marketing, companies focus on developing content that presents insights and solutions for the potential customer and helps the customer in finding success with the product.

An important component of inbound marketing is the management of personalization. This management strategy produces strong stock returns with the top quartile personalization leaders beating the sp500 by 15%.

Strategy

In this section, I would like to try to describe HubSpot’s strategy and how it positions versus a CRM giant called Salesforce (CRM).

Provide CRM that enables inbound based marketing, targeting SMBs. HubSpot helps the client company to develop, publicize and personalize content across email, social media, and websites. This process allows the company to better connect with customers, develop a richer brand voice and reach potential customers. HubSpot targets SMBs focusing on intuitive interfaces that don’t require a lot of technical knowledge to engage with. SMBs have less access to advanced talent. Easy to use software is a plus in this segment. In contrast, large enterprises require more complex and advanced vertical customization and specific custom-made workflows that Salesforce allows, but the advanced features come at a much higher price tag.

Allow SMBs to conduct personalization at a lower cost. Personalization is a new trend affecting all companies. Usually, only the larger enterprises have access to powerful personalization, via advanced data gathering techniques, enrichment of data with third-party suppliers, and centralized databases where data has a single source of truth and data storage is highly scalable. Additionally, these large enterprises use highly technical machine learning to improve, via multi channels, customer engagement, conversion, cross-selling and customer lifetime value. HubSpot enables personalization with medium intensity yet can bring results to the HubSpot users. There are several features that absorb data important for personalization, such as HubSpot website tracking, email engagement and customer conversations with the company. With this data, the HubSpot’s customer can conduct personalized emails, SMS and website offers, improving its conversion and engagement.

Provide an intuitive, effective gen AI with transparent pricing. HubSpot is engaging with gen AI to boost productivity and personalization. Gen AI allows the customer to generate content more effectively, tailoring emails and SMS to each customer. Its gen AI agents further improve personalization by coaching the sales agent prior to the conversation with the potential customer. The gen AI agents also enhance the core platform analyses, such as understanding brand image, segmenting the customers and understanding customer sentiment. Compared with Salesforce’s Einstein GPT, HubSpot is known to have a more transparent pricing and the user requires less technical abilities to use, improving HubSpot moat around SMBs.

Overall, I like HubSpot’s strategy and the large customer base it has developed along the way is impressive, with more than 200k customers. The new gen AI looks very useful and should increase ARPU, providing much needed growth for the next few years.

Financials

On the gross margins, HubSpot is much more profitable than Salesforce, showing better economics in terms of value, price and cost of the solution.

In terms of FCF/Employed Capital, HubSpot is much more efficient than Salesforce. Salesforce is highly invested in acquisitions to grow while HubSpot has been growing and developing solutions in-house, for now, showing superior capital management and more R&D strength compared to the CRM giant.

From a valuation perspective, HubSpot is much more valued. I believe HubSpot’s superior valuation is fair given the massive outperformance of FCF/EC and significant outperformance in revenue growth.

Opportunities

HubSpot gen AI allows for advanced and optimized Content Creation. This is an important dimension of personalization consequently creating better communication with the potential customers. Personalization of content can occur in mails, sales agent communication, SMS and personalized offers while the customer is browsing the company website. This HubSpot’s gen AI agent also enables content creation to strengthen the company’s brand image, across landing pages, videos and blog posts.

In the earnings call the HubSpot’s management said this:

It has been a great year for Content Hub with rapid growth driven by AI innovation. We added video capabilities to our popular Content Remix feature and launched Content Agent that helps create and personalize content. Customers are quickly adopting these AI features to create landing pages, videos and blog posts and they're seeing immediate value.

For example, Franchise Brokers association, our customer, who connects great leaders with franchise opportunities, increase content production by 250% and improve organic lead generation by 216% using tools like our Blog Writer and Content Remix. The level of innovation with AI features in Content Hub has accelerated growth and the Attach rate to Marketing Hub is Strong at nearly 50%.

Risks

There are multiple smaller CRMs that should provide good value despite having less advanced features. HubSpot’s growth is highly impacted by these smaller competitors’ offers.

Klaviyo (KVYO) is very powerful in personalization, specializing in strong machine learning with a very big data moat to manage its SMB clients tailored communications around SMS and mail. This company might expand and cover more cases of personalization, such as personalized landing pages and content development affecting HubSpot’s value proposition.

Since HubSpot works mostly with SMBs, macro is an important factor. SMBs have higher sensitivity to macro and should optimize IT spending in case of an economic slowdown, increasing HUBS’ churn rate and reducing its net revenue retention.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. The initial revenue value is the sell-side analysts estimate for 2025. The starting free cash flow margin is 19%. Then the margin expands to 24.79% in 2034. To generate the terminal multiple, I assumed a multiple of 11 that dilutes 1% throughout the ten years, resulting in a multiple of 10. Since I’m not doing the math per share and I wish to keep the model simple, I prefer to consider that the dilution affects the total market cap (It doesn’t, it just affects the price per share). The growth is balanced to obtain the current market cap.

The model outputs a growth that starts at 15.8% and then decreases 5% every year until reaching 9.9%. If we look at sell-side long-term analysts' expectations that, usually, are optimistic, HubSpot looks somewhat fairly valued.

Conclusion

HubSpot provides a platform that allows SMBs and large enterprises to consolidate sales, marketing, content creation and service management. I prefer HubSpot over Salesforce due to higher expected growth, higher operational efficiency and in-house product development. However, HUBS faces personalization competition from the likes of Klaviyo. The competition might increase capping HubSpot upside. HUBS looks fairly valued and posed for revenue growth returns. In my portfolio, usually, I buy companies with very strong long-term growth prospects, high-quality and fair valuation. I don’t think HUBS fits the high growth for longer requirement, and thus I rate it a hold.

data.title: McDonald's Corporation: A Good Buy At Current Levels
data.description:
Investment Thesis

McDonald's Corporation (NYSE:MCD) should benefit from recovery from the E. coli fallout and, as its recently launched McValue platform gains traction. International sales should also benefit from the easing of boycotts in the Middle East post the recent peace deal as well as the recovery in Chinese sales. In addition, similar to the U.S. market, the value offerings in the global markets should also help revenue growth. In the long term, the company's focus on increasing its loyalty customers on its digital platform should help in revenue expansion as they spend more compared to non-digital customers. Further, the opening of new restaurants globally should also benefit growth.

On a margin front, volume leverage and the non-recurrence of spending related to regaining customer trust after the E. coli breakout should support margins. The company's stock is trading at lower than its historical levels with a 2.28% dividend yield. Given its good growth prospects and favorable valuation, I rate the stock a buy.

McDonald's Corporation Revenue Analysis and Outlook

The company reported good comp sales growth from FY21 to FY23, driven by the recovery from COVID-19 impact and good execution of the Accelerating the Arches initiative. However, FY24 was a relatively tough year as the company faced headwinds from an E. coli breakout and an inflationary environment impacting customers.

In the fourth quarter of FY24, sales declined due to lower demand driven by tight consumer spending and the negative impact from the E. coli outbreak linked to slivered onions in the U.S. As a result, sales declined by 0.3% Y/Y to $6.388 bn. However, the comp sales were positive as the decline in the US comp sales was offset by strength in international markets.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about the company's growth prospects. Last quarter, the company was seeing good growth trends in the U.S. in early October with positive check as well as traffic growth. Its total comp sales was up mid single digits in the first three weeks of October before food safety issues related to the E. coli outbreak negatively impacted the sales. Thanks to the company's rapid and transparent handling of the situation and re-engaging customers, its sales started seeing a good sequential recovery after bottoming in early November. According to management, the company's comp growth turned slightly positive in December, and this sequential growth momentum continued in 1Q 25. This continued momentum bodes well for the company's growth in the U.S. moving forward.

While the broader macros remain tough, the company is doing a good job in terms of focusing on value offering to gain market share. In January, the company launched McValue Platform. On this platform, the company has introduced the new Buy One, Add One for $1 offer in addition to the already popular $5 McDeal offer. Further, the company is introducing even more offers in its App, like free medium fries with a $1 purchase every Friday in 2025 and a free McCrispy chicken sandwich for new app users. According to management, the traction for the McValue platform is going as expected, and the company's focus on value offering should help it gain share and post growth in the U.S. despite a tough macro environment.

On the international side, the company's comparable sales have turned positive, and I believe the worst is behind us. The company, along with several other U.S. brands, was facing a boycott in the Middle East, and the recent peace deal should help sentiments to some extent. Even the company's peers are seeing better trends, and Yum! Brands (YUM) reported that KFC mid-east traffic has returned to pre-conflict levels in December. Yum! Brands also reported that Chinese sales are improving sequentially. Another major international chain, Starbucks (SBUX), saw its international comp sales down Y/Y last quarter, but it was also better than consensus expectations. Looking at the results and commentary of McDonald's and its major peers, I am optimistic about the company's growth prospects outside of the U.S. Further, similar to the U.S., the company is emphasizing its value offering in the international markets as well and some of the noteworthy campaigns included the Hot Ones partnership and €4 Happy Meal promotion in France, and the $1 coffee promotion and the Grinch meal campaign in Canada. I believe the generally improving conditions in the global market coupled with the company's value messaging bodes well for its sales in international markets looking forward.

The company's digital ecosystem, including its loyalty program, also bodes well for medium to long-term growth both in the U.S. and globally. The company's app has 170 mn 90-day active users across 60 markets who contributed ~ $30 bn in systemwide sales. The company aims to increase this to 250mn active users and $45 bn in systemwide sales by the end of 2027.

According to management, loyalty customers on the company's digital ecosystem spend more than non-digital counterparts, and the company's focus on increasing app subscribers should help comparable sales in the longer run.

In addition to increasing same-restaurant sales, the company's revenues should also benefit from new restaurant openings. In 2025, the company is planning to open 2200 global gross openings, including 1000 in China. In the medium term, the company is planning 4-5% annual unit growth with a goal to reach 50K units by 2027, which should nicely add to the revenue growth.

So, I am optimistic about the company's revenue growth prospects.

McDonald's Corporation Margin Analysis and Outlook

In the fourth quarter of FY24, the adjusted operating margin declined by 90 bps Y/Y. The U.S. segment was in a tough spot with its operating margin declining by 240 bps Y/Y due to an unfavorable mix as the high margin Quarter Pounder sales declined as a result of the food safety incident. In addition, increased investments to regain customer trust also impacted margins.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about the company's margin prospects. The company's franchise model, with ~95% of its restaurants franchised, gives it a good stability in terms of margins. Last quarter was a bit unique as E.coli breakout and consequent investment to regain customer trust impacted margins. However, this is not going to recur moving forward, helping margins.

The company-operated restaurants' margins were also impacted as a result of lower revenues. However, given the positive revenue outlook, we should see a good leverage on increased sales. Management has guided for a slight improvement in margins for 2025, and I believe it should achieve that with the help of volume leverage and non-recurrence of E. coli-related expenses.

Valuations and Rating

MCD is trading at 25.11x FY25 consensus EPS estimates of $12.34 and 23.23x FY26 consensus EPS estimates of $13.35. This is a discount versus its 5-year average forward P/E of 26.59x. The company is a good long-term growth story with 4-5% annual unit growth, benefits from increasing loyalty members, and value positioning, which helps it gain share even during the tough consumer environment. This should help the company post mid to high single-digit revenue growth in the long run. A slight improvement in margins, coupled with share buyback, can take total long-term EPS growth to high single digits CAGR.

While the company's P/E has seen some re-rating in the recent weeks, especially after the earnings, I believe E. coli concerns fading and international markets improving can help further re-rating, and the stock can trade in-line with its 5-year historical levels. Applying the 5-year average P/E of 26.59x P/E to FY26 EPS of $13.35x, we get a one-year forward target price of $354.98 or ~14.5% upside from the current levels. Add to that ~2.28% dividend; we get mid to high teens percentage total return potentials. Hence, I have a buy rating on the stock.

Risks

  • The company is exposed to currency risks and adverse FX movements, given its global exposure.
  • If the Middle East peace deal fails and the conflict is reignited, the western brands may face boycott again, negatively impacting McDonald's growth.
  • The company has so far seen good growth in its digital subscribers and their engagement, but if it fails to sustain this positive trend, its sales growth may be adversely impacted.

data.title: Now Isn't The Time To Buy MTU Aero Engines
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

Over the past few years, I've gotten more into the aerospace and defense segment, specifically writing about it. New companies which are both in defense and aerospace were added to my coverage spectrum over the past 2-3 years, with prominent examples that have generated very impressive RoR for me and my portfolio. Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEF) is one good example. One company I haven't covered, at least not on SA, is MTU Aero Engines (OTCPK:MTUAF). That changes today.

The valuation currently seen in the European market tells me that with some small exceptions, value/attractiveness is mostly found in European majors. Often French or German ones, or Central European at the very least. While there are large-cap turnaround stories in other markets, it's here that I currently see the most value, the most bang for my buck. That's also why I invest mostly here at the time.

Just over the past week, I have put more money into many French businesses. But one of the companies I added to my list is German-listed MTU, and here I will provide you with a basic thesis, my current view on the company and where it might be going, and why I won't be investing in the company - at least not at this particular time.

MTU Aero Engines - Attractive, but requires a certain valuation not found today

So, MTU. Some basic facts to begin with, as I start presenting you with the results of my weeks-long fact checks and delving. The company supplies engine components and repair services, maintenance services, and overhaul services for engines and aerospace components - as its name suggests. This means that if the company has, or has the chance to get, a good market share of the rising segment of the narrow-body aircraft that we've seen over the past decade, the company is set to benefit from this.

Anyone covering the sector or even flying frequently can't have missed the rise and the airline preference for mid-size narrow-body assets. They're cheaper to maintain and more economical to run. Also, the current asset lifecycle/age implies that over 15% of the current fleet of planes is in excess of 20 years old, which doesn't just benefit Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) and Boeing (BA), one of which I invest in (Airbus), but also repair shops like MTU. In fact, current expectations according to my own forecast using data from the industry imply that in excess of 25,000 airplanes are going to be delivered by 2035 - and this is a massive amount.

So where does MTU fit into all of this?

Well, the company has a 15%+ market share for components for the very popular geared turbofan engines from Pratt & Whitney - this will be a driver of top-line results here because GTF engines represent almost the majority of engine installations in the popular Airbus 320neo, the most popular narrow-body aircraft currently on the market in terms of orders. Over 25,000 of the planes are expected deliveries by 2035, and that means that MTU has a share of repairs, maintenance, and overhauls for its market share in that segment. Also, I expect that the amount of installations of these engines in narrow-body planes will go up.

MTU is a combination of spare parts, service, and ancillary operations. In fact, spare parts make up less than 20% of the company's top line, but over 60% of the company's bottom line. Because I expect newer engines to be more heavy on components and spare parts, not less, I expect the company's growth potential to be higher going forward, not less.

So, MTU is around 16-18% of the GTF maintenance and service program. I expect this to have the potential to rise to 20% in the next few years, maybe even more. Why? Because MTU has a partnership with the manufacturer of these engines, Pratt & Whitney. The fact that the current global fleet is aging rapidly, and the fact that the requirements for operation in most of the Western world are increasing in complexity and demand means that there is a forecast of growth in aftermarket and spare parts - and these are high-margin industries, due to the specifics of the technical requirements for these parts, as well as the expertise to install them.

Furthermore, for once, China and Asia aren't really a risk to the company, because MTU comes with good Asia exposure because of a JV, the MTU Zhuhai.

As you can see above though, the company is more than just maintenance. While revenues for the commercial MRO are more than 65%, the company also has a military arm and a Commercial OEM arm. I believe the former of these is actually one of the reasons the company is flying so high at this time in terms of its share price.

The current outlook for the company's financials, based on what is above and what we can see here, is good. MTU is forecasting ongoing demand recovery continuing following the COVID-19 slump, with revenues in excess of €8B, and EBIT at over 10% margin of that revenue. Specific improvements are currently expected in Commercial MRO, spare parts, Commercial OE, and military - so all segments. I would personally take a somewhat more cautious view of the military arm. Once the war in Ukraine is resolved, I expect the company's share price to take a bit of a nose-dive, as I expect most of the European defense majors to go - though not as low as they once traded. So that's one reason I remain cautious.

One reason I don't remain cautious, is the company's fundamentals, because these are rock-solid. The company has a goal to never go beyond 1.5x net debt/EBITDA, and to pay out a target of 40% of adjusted net income in company dividends. And financials are supportive of the excellent company positioning, from a fundamental perspective.

In 2023, the company had a huge EBITDA hit due to an issue with its PW1100 fleet management - but aside from this, trends have only gone up. For 2024, the company's revenue is up double digits, as is adjusted EBIT, net income and other Financial KPIs.

Going forward, the company is set to report full-year results within a month. While I expect that the 4Q and 2024E results will bring about the growth currently forecasted, about 19% adjusted EPS Growth, this growth is not even coming close to matching the post-COVID-19 recovery numbers which, In my view, were central to the outperformance we've seen from the company in the last 1-2 years. It's my view that the company will go from an average annual EPS growth of 40-50% on a 1-2 year basis between 2022-2023, to around 14-15% in the next few years, and this isn't yet priced into the stock at these valuation levels. These forecasts also don't take into consideration further risks of technical issues with the GTF engines, which would of course impact company earnings (though for 4Q and 2024, I don't consider this to be likely). As such, I don't see the 4Q24 earnings impact my overall thesis and the current estimates for the full year with revenues over the €7.4B mark and an EBITDA margin of about 17.5% to be valid here.

In terms of fundamental risks, what I see in MTU is mostly related to 1-2 things. First, the company could be impacted by further production and technical issues from GTF engines (which have already happened and could happen again). This has to do with GTF engine reliability, among other things, which also leads me to the risk that there is a risk in the industry of longer-term contracts to combat this - this would go against a company like MTU (through pricing risks, at least if these issues aren't fixed). Also, ironically, if airlines and fleet managers decide to retire older assets earlier, this could mean a hit for aftermarket, repair, and overhaul service revenues. So it's a careful balance of risks, upsides, and downsides that needs to be taken into the context of valuation - and that is where I do not currently think that the company presents and attractive picture.

Let me show you what I mean.

MTU Aero Engines - at any conservative P/E, the company's 5-6-year average here is below 8% per year, even with dividends

When I invest, I look at a combination of two things. The historically averaged P/E or similar KPI puts into context future growth and the likelihood of that future growth happening, and peer valuation to see where the sector goes. Yield presents an attractive picture If I consider the company a so-called "income investment". At 0.6% yield at this time, MTU is not an income investment in any way, unfortunately.

The first is, historical averages and growth. MTU is currently forecasting, and so are analysts, a double-digit rate of EPS growth between 11-13% per year until 2026E on the back of good business trends, as mentioned in the past. I believe this to be an accurate estimate for the most part.

However, when it comes to an inarguably cyclical defense and aerospace company like this, even one in MRO, I say the longer-term P/E's are more accurate in deciphering the future possibilities. That means using a 19.9x P/E, which is the 20-year average for the company (Source: F.A.S.T graphs Paywalled Link).

And using this 20-year average when forecasting a 12.5% growth rate, that means at €335/share you're getting an annualized RoR of 7.44%. I don't believe the risk/reward parameters in this case to be attractively positioned for an investment based on this way of viewing the company. There are too many "what-ifs", both sentimental-based and based on actual numbers and trends that could impact the company, such as GTF complications. So from this way of viewing it, despite my saying that MTU certainly has a moat for its services given its relationships, contracts, and history, the company is not in a position for purchase here.

Second way of seeing, peers. We have GE Aerospace (GE), RTX (RTX), and maybe Rolls-Royce as well, which I have mentioned. Out of this peer group, there isn't in my mind a very evident "Buy". The best we can see for RTX and GE are companies that are somewhat attractive or fairly valued. The best out of the group would be, in my mind, RTX, which at below $130/share would be at something approaching a "Buy", also having a yield close to 2%. But RTX also comes with a somewhat different risk and sales profile, where I consider MTU to be better.

Overall, I would argue that because of industry fragmentation and risks, I would not put the company at anything above a €270/share PT - and that's already including a premium for the expected increase and growth in flight hours and interest. I expect 11-12% EBIT margins, as the share of spare parts to the sales profile improves, and we're also likely to see growth of this margin towards the 13-14% level in 2028E owing to the same trend. But that's where the positive part stops, and that's why 18-19x P/E is the highest I'm willing to go. In short, I believe the market "has it right", and put the company at an 18x P/E in order to get that 15% annualized forecast.

That means I missed out on investing in MTU a little over a year ago - but that's fine, I'll wait for the company to go down again.

The relevant ADR in this thesis is MTUAF. It's a 1:1 Share ADR, which means that the equivalent PT of €270/share comes to around $283/share.

My thesis for the company is as follows:

Thesis

  • MTU Aero Engines is possibly with the exception of Rolls-Royce, which I have shares in, one of the more attractive aerospace and defense companies in Europe when it comes to sales mix, due to a high share of aftermarket/MRO sales and operations.
  • At an attractive valuation, I view MTU Aero Engines as almost a "must have" due to the exposure to the growing narrow-body fleet, contracts, and the projected increase in flight hours over the next 10 or so years.
  • However, the current valuation for the company has been inflated by things like the defense sector, macroeconomics, and the current market, and at the current valuation, I view this company as a "No go".
  • An attractive valuation for the company in my estimate would come to about €270/share, representing an 18x P/E with an upside of around 20x. The yield isn't high, so this is mostly a capital appreciation play.
  • My current rating for the company is a "HOLD".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €270/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of February 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hormel: Worth Considering At A 4% Yield (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Consumer Staples sector has done fine to begin 2025, up 3.6%, but the economically insensitive area has lagged the S&P 500 once again. Despite high-fliers like Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST), most household-name Staples stocks have merely trodden water over the last handful of months.

Today, I’m revisiting a notable laggard, Hormel Foods (NYSE:HRL). I was bearish on the stock back in November of 2022, highlighting fundamental and technical challenges facing the now $16 billion market cap Packaged Foods and Meats industry firm. I’m upgrading HRL from a sell to a hold after a more than 35% decline over the past 27 months, losing more than 90 percentage points to the S&P 500 over that span.

Headwinds are still in place, such as uncertainty in the pork market, but HRL’s dividend yield is now above 4% and free cash flow appears sufficient to sustain that yield. Still, the valuation is not all that compelling, even with EPS growth expected.

Consumer Staples Lagging in 2025 So Far

Back in December, Hormel reported a weak set of quarterly results. Q3 GAAP EPS came in at $0.40, missing the Wall Street consensus estimate by two pennies while revenue of $3.14 billion, down 2% from the same period a year earlier, was a slight $2.6 million miss. Shares inched higher by 0.5% in the session that followed, and we’ve seen the stock hold a key technical level lately that I will offer color on later in the article.

Looking closer at the quarter, HRL tallied record cash from operations at $1.3 billion and its capex dipped to $256 million. So, free cash flow was helped by those figures; I also like that the company returned $615 million to shareholders.

HRL continues to face challenges such as weak meat markets, particularly in turkey, and the Planters brand has been a drag for several quarters now. For FY 2025, the management team forecasted net sales of $11.9-$12.2 billion with adjusted EPS of $1.58-$1.72. They are hopeful for a positive turnaround in Planters and less volatile pork supply and prices.

On the earnings outlook, analysts are not all that upbeat about Hormel’s prospects, but operating EPS is seen rising 4% this year and accelerating to an 8-10% growth rate from the out year through FY 2027. Profitability trends are decent, with free cash flow being the standout right now.

Nevertheless, the sell side has turned more bearish on the stock over the past 90 days, considering seven EPS downgrades over the past 90 days compared with zero upgrades. But for the first time in the last 10 years, the dividend yield is above 4%.

Hormel: Revenue & EPS Forecasts, Earnings Revision Trends

HRL: Dividend Yield Has Climbed to Above 4%

On valuation, if we assume $1.70 of non-GAAP EPS over the next 12 months and apply a 17 multiple, then shares should trade near $29, close to where HRL is today. I assert that a 17 P/E is right because the EPS growth rate looks to normalize around 9%.

The resulting PEG is a bit below 2, in line with the market average. A single-digit bottom-line growth rate simply does not warrant a mid-20s earnings multiple (the stock’s 5-year average). Shares also trade in line with the sector median price-to-sales ratio.

Hormel: Mixed Valuation Metrics

Key risks for Hormel include uncertainty in the turkey and pork wholesale markets; price volatility is obviously a concern, but supply/demand dynamics may be in flux due to geopolitical risks and animal pandemics. In general, lower cost inflation would be a boon and a healthy global economy would help this geographically diverse business.

Higher market interest rates and ongoing weakness in Planters are problem spots. Hormel plans on increasing advertising investments, so those will have to pan out for operational performance to improve.

Hormel: Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q1 2025 earnings date of Thursday, February 27 BMO with a conference call later that morning. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

The options market prices in a 5% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle, expiring soonest after the Q4 report later this month, per data from Option Research & Technology Services (ORATS). Seeking Alpha shows a consensus EPS estimate of $0.39 on revenue of $2.95 billion; those numbers would compare to $0.41 and $3.00 billion a year ago.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares being near fair value in my estimation, HRL’s technical view is very weak at first blush. But take a look at where the stock appears to have paused in the broad downtrend – the low from a year ago at $28.51 has held as support for now. The stock is in a precarious position, of course, so there’s a real risk that a bearish breakdown transpires. If so, a measured move downside price target to $20 would be in play based on the $8.50 range/consolidation since Q4 of 2023.

Moreover, the long-term 200-day moving average is negatively sloped, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend and the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is near oversold conditions after brief spikes that proved to be bear market rallies. While support is near the current price, resistance is way up at $37 and at the downtrend line off the 2022 peak.

HRL: Shares in a Long-Term Downtrend, $28-$29 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on Hormel. I see the Consumer Staples stock as fairly priced but with a very high dividend yield that may attract investors if Treasury rates drop. Its chart is bearish, but near-term support is in play.

data.title: Biogen Q4 Earnings Review: Why I'm Calling Time On This 'Powder Puff' Pharma
data.description:
Investment Overview

Biogen (NASDAQ:BIIB) reported its Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings on Wednesday, 12th February. I last covered the company for Seeking Alpha after it had announced its Q3 earnings, which, despite out-performing street estimates on revenues and earnings per share ("EPS"), triggered a market sell-off - from $182 per share, to ~$175 per share, on light full-year guidance.

Despite being forewarned, Wall Street dumped Biogen stock again after Wednesday's Q4 results. Quarterly revenues came in at $2.5bn, up 3% year-on-year, and GAAP EPS came to $1.83, up 7% year-on-year. Non-GAAP EPS was $3.44, up 17%. For the full-year, revenues amounted to $9.7bn, down 2% year-on-year, while GAAP diluted EPS was up 40%, to $11.18, and non-GAAP EPS $16.47, up 12% year-on-year.

In terms of guidance for 2025, Biogen forecast for an EPS of $15.25-$16.25, with total revenues expected to decline by a mid-single digit percentage at constant currency, which management puts down to declining revenues within its Multiple Sclerosis franchise, "partially offset by revenue growth from newly launched products".

Analysis - No Sign Of Meaningful Leqembi Revenues

I have been posting on Biogen for Seeking Alpha since 2021, covering the controversial approval of its Alzheimer's drug, Aduhelm, in June 2021, which sent its stock price soaring from $283 per share, to nearly $400 per share (I held Biogen stock during that period, then sold it).

As we now know, Aduhelm was not only a controversial drug - being potentially unsafe, and barely generating any signs of efficacy in clinical studies, - it was a complete commercial failure, and was removed from the market after making just $300k of sales.

The only other time Biogen stock has spiked was in September 2022, when, alongside Japanese Pharma Eisai, its development partner in the Alzheimer's treatment field, the company shared data from a second AD drug, lecanemab, showing that in its Phase 3 study it had "reduced clinical decline on the global cognitive and functional scale (CDR-SB) by 27% compared to those on placebo at 18 months".

In May 2023, after lecanemab had been granted accelerated approval for commercial use, under the brand name Leqembi, Biogen stock traded at >$315 per share, and the drug received full approval in July 2023. In 2024, however, Biogen reported just $60m of collaboration revenues related to the drug (revenues and costs are split 50/50 between the two companies), despite Eisai's forecast that the drug could achieve $7bn of revenues by 2030.

The reality is that Leqembi has a similar mechanism of action to Aduhelm, targeting amyloid beta ("sticky" clumps of protein, the removal of which has been associated with improvement in a patient's condition) that presents the same safety issues as Aduhelm, related to potentially fatal amyloid related imaging abnormalities ("ARIA") requiring users of the drug to submit to regular brain scans. Furthermore, the drug may be inferior to Eli Lilly's (LLY) Kisunla, approved last year (there are significant doubts around whether Kisunla will drive meaningful revenues, also, given it targets amyloid beta removal).

Since the approval of Leqembi, Biogen's stock has retreated in value to its current value of $138 per share, down >55%.

Multiple Sclerosis, Other Key Drug Franchises In Decline - New Drug Approvals Offer Scant Hope

Biogen's main revenue driver for years has been its multiple sclerosis franchise, but revenues within the franchise have declined from $8.5bn in 2019, to $4.4bn in 2024, although the division still accounted for 45% of all revenues in 2024.

The patent expiries of Tecfidera, Plegridy, and Tysabri have been chiefly responsible for the declines, and although Vumerity, approved in 2019, is viewed as a long-term replacement, it may be the case it never achieves "blockbuster" revenues with rivals such as Swiss Pharma giant Novatis' >$3bn per annum selling Kesimpta, Roche's (OTCQX:RHHBY) Ocrevus, and TG Therapeutics' (TGTX) Briumvi all competing for market share.

Meanwhile within Biogen's Rare Disease portfolio, Spinal Muscular Atrophy therapy Spinraza's revenues have fallen from in every year since 2019, to $1.6bn in 2024, while recently launched Qalsody, indicated for Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis ("ALS"), earned just $32m of revenues, and Skyclarys, a drug indicated for Friedreich's Ataxia ("FA"), acquired as part of Biogen's $7.3bn acquisition of Reata Pharmaceuticals, contributed $383m of revenues, showing no growth between Q3 and Q4.

Within neuromuscular, Zurzuvae, a depression med developed in collaboration with Sage Therapeutics (SAGE), is another crushing disappointment, refused approval in Major Depressive Disorder ("MDD") and eking out just $72m of revenues in 2024. Within the biosimilar division, Rheumatoid Arthritis drug Benepali led the way with $479m of revenues, up 9% year-on-year, but the division as a whole grew just 3% annually and is nowhere being an answer to Biogen's problems.

Elsewhere, Biogen does reasonably well out of its collaboration revenues from Roche in relation to Ocrevus, earning $1.34bn in 2024, up 6%, but Rituxan/Gazyva collaboration revenues fell 4%. Contract manufacturing, royalty and other revenues were down 27%, to $653m.

Pipeline "Promise" Is Thoroughly Underwhelming

Biogen shares the following overview of its pipeline in its Q4 earnings presentation:

There are no product approval highlights to look forward to in 2025, and none in 2026 or 2027, either - it could even be the case Biogen secures no product approvals between now and the end of the decade. It would not surprise me - Biogen is that kind of company.

The areas that Biogen is targeting - systemic lupus erythematosus ("SLE"), IgA nephropathy (IgAN), are tough disease indications to treat, and also subject to intense competition - I would put my faith in the likes of Travere Therapeutics (TVTX) in IgAN, AstraZeneca (AZN), Gilead Sciences (GILD) and several other smaller biotechs in SLE before I would put any faith in Biogen pipeline candidates litifilimab, felzartamab, or Dapirolizumab pegol.

Litfilimab did not garner a mention on Biogen's earnings call, no data was shared in relation to felzartamab, and pegol was described as "only the third agent with a positive global Phase III study in SLE" - not exactly words to set pulses racing on Wall Street.

Sage Acquisition Debacle Sums Up A Company On The Outs

If any further evidence is still required as to why - after three straight posts assigning Biogen a "Buy" rating, I am switching my stance to a "sell", the story of Biogen's attempted buyout of Sage Therapeutics may provide it.

Biogen and Sage have collaborated for years - a collaboration that has been a colossal disappointment for both companies, with only Zurzuvae to show for it, and a host of study failures - Sage stock (which I own a small amount of) is down 90% on a five-year basis.

You might think the companies knew each other well enough and have felt enough egg landing on their faces to be able to hold cordial talks around Biogen potentially acquiring Sage, and putting management out of its misery, but in reality, Sage has brought a lawsuit against Biogen after receiving an "unsolicited" buyout offer.

Biogen's offer was to buyout the shares of Sage it does not already own for a sum of ~$469m, or $7.22 per share - barely a premium to its current traded price, which could be described as an insultingly low offer. A Sage spokesperson has been quoted as saying:

Sage has filed necessary paperwork with the Delaware court seeking a temporary restraining order to preserve our rights and enforce the standstill provision previously agreed to with Biogen

Concluding Thoughts - I Can No Longer Find A Single Reason To Get Excited About Biogen Stock - Sell!

Amidst all of this debacle, Biogen's CEO Christopher A. Viehbacher received $30.5m in pay and stock option awards in his first year on the job in 2022, having replaced Michel Vounatsos, who fell on his sword after the Aduhelm situation got so bad hospitals were refusing to let Biogen sales execs from entering the building.

In fairness, Viehbacher received only $4m in 2023, and the point of this post is not to denigrate Biogen's management or staff, although its hard to avoid the conclusion that Biogen embodies most of what the public - and Wall Street - dislike most about Big Pharma. Bad, overpriced products, endless study failures, lack of innovation, no pipeline promise, billions in wasted R&D, failing share price, no dividend, bad PR.

Biogen reported $2.4bn of cash as of the end of 2024, and only $4.5bn of long-term debt, so, at least the company is not as heavily indebted as other Pharma companies.

Although the company has been struggling, in previous posts I have always arrived at the conclusion that by most investment metrics, its stock looks cheap. Price to sales ratio of just over 2x is far lower than average for a Big Pharma, and forward price to earnings, ~8.7x - also significantly lower than average.

Consider the valuations of other Pharmas driving revenues close to the $10bn mark. Vertex Pharma (VRTX), $11bn revenues in 2024, market cap valuation $118bn. Regeneron (REGN), ~$14bn revenues, $74bn market cap. However, I suspect the reality is that these Pharmas are able to show Wall Street that they mean business, and have long-term plans, products in their pipelines, and astute and caring management that can help establish them amongst the biggest and most relevant Pharma's globally in the long term.

In 2024, Bausch Health (BHC) will likely earn ~$9bn of revenues, yet its market cap valuation is $2.2bn, because it is crippled by debt and Wall Street clearly does not believe there is an easy way out of the hole the company has dug for itself.

Biogen has no such debt concerns, it feels as though the company is simply treading water, with no products or plans to get excited about. Even though this is a company that ought to stand every chance of being successful, after following Biogen for so many years and witnessing almost no positive momentum be established, I think its valuation may well end up in the same place as Bausch Health's - it may be time to break this business up, although it is not obvious what assets would be attractive to any company.

data.title: Microsoft: Owning The AI Stack Helps To Counter The DeepSeek Threat
data.description:
When I last covered Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) in February 2023, I had a hold position, mainly because I had found C3.ai (AI) to be a better investment for exposure to the artificial intelligence theme. I got it wrong; the software giant delivered over 25% gains, mainly because I had failed to anticipate the advantages conferred on its products because of its sizable investment in OpenAI. As a result, it owns the AI stack.

Nearly two years later, Chinese AI startup DeepSeek's LLM (large language model) outperforming OpenAI's equivalent for certain benchmarks has caused Microsoft's stock to suffer more than peer hyperscaler Amazon (AMZN) as charted below. This thesis aims to show that such underperformance may not be justified and that instead, the software giant is better positioned to harvest the benefits of AI diffusing across industries.

I start by highlighting the risks posed to AI infrastructure builders, including Microsoft, because of the distillation effect instilled by DeepSeek.

Understanding the AI Distillation Effect Instilled by DeepSeek as the focus turns to monetization

First, putting things into context, Microsoft suffered as a broader sell-off impacting the Mag7, represented here by the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) since around January 28 as charted below. Thus, holding NVIDIA (NVDA) was also impacted as the Chinese company showed that with optimizations at the software algorithm level, namely an improvement in the mixture-of-experts training, it was possible to build AI models with lower-performance GPUs.

To achieve this feat, DeepSeek may have used training data from ChatGPT in a process called distillation whereby knowledge is transferred from larger to smaller models allowing achievement of similar performance but using less computational costs. Now, even if this means that it is not competing on an equal footing with OpenAI (in terms of computing power used), the Chinese company has played an eye-opener role in showing that massive data center build-outs using NVIDIA's cutting-edge GPUs are not prime requisites to train an AI model to achieve top performance.

Thus, it is likely for companies looking to build their AI models to consider options like optimizing their algorithms instead of resorting to sheer brute-force GPU scaling, potentially impacting orders from the semiconductor giant and the related data center supply chain. Other potential losers from DeepSeek’s breakthrough are those actively building data center capacity, including Microsoft, Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Amazon at the cost of billions of dollars.

Microsoft's Stock suffered more due to the OpenAI Partnership

To support my point, Microsoft's stock slumping despite beating second-quarter fiscal 2025 earnings by $0.11, hints that investors may be looking beyond purely financial performance, to capital investment made on AI. Thus, after spending around $55.7 billion in FY-24, Microsoft should invest about $80 billion to build AI infrastructures during this fiscal year to train and deploy LLMs and host cloud-based applications globally.

Thus, in the case of distillation becomes widespread, the danger is AI infrastructures being built by Microsoft and other hyperscalers may not attract customers as planned. In this connection, the sell-off of AI-linked stocks suggests the market is recalibrating its expectations to factor in this scenario.

Still, the fact that Microsoft's stock suffered relatively more can be explained by DeepSeek-related cost improvements lowering the barriers to entry for model training, a process that normally consumes significant computational resources. As such, this paves the way for more affordable AI solutions developed by mainstream service providers, instead of most developments being confined to tech giants. Moreover, since DeepSeek is based on open source, it can rapidly become more widely accessible to developers, posing a threat to Microsoft’s exclusive partnership with OpenAI by allowing more organizations to build advanced AI applications without relying on proprietary, and premium-priced services.

Thus, as open-source projects mature, they could grind into Microsoft's competitive moat, forcing it to compete on features like pricing, especially if developers manage to tailor open-source models to cater to their specific needs in a way that exceeds the performance of proprietary solutions.

Microsoft's Advantages of Owning the AI Stack and Having an Integrated Ecosystem

However, firstly, unlike the IT solutions providers, which subscribe to OpenAI's models, Microsoft owns and controls the AI stack given its nearly $14 billion investment in Sam Altman's company. Also, while AI distillation can weaken its model exclusivity, Microsoft has deep integration with OpenAI, giving it first access to GPT-4 Turbo and future model releases. Also, the software giant can boost R&D to ensure that its offerings remain at the cutting edge.

Secondly, Microsoft can counter the open-source threat by focusing on security, compliance, and support, or areas where proprietary products often have an edge. This is exemplified in the server O.S. space, where despite Linux being widely adopted, many large enterprises still rely on Windows-based servers.

Thirdly, the company could come up with alternative pricing strategies including consumption-based charging depending on usage like Salesforce (CRM) has done with Agentforce AI. Furthermore, DeepSeek R1’s availability in the model catalog for Azure AI Foundry alongside 1,800 others sets the stage for potential monetization namely through integrating open-source developments with its proprietary components. To this end, with its ownership and support of the open-source version control software platform GitHub, Microsoft can drive the adoption of its proprietary enhancements.

Fourth, Microsoft’s integrated ecosystem is synonymous with strength.

In this respect, 365 Copilot, which is an artificial intelligence add-on for Microsoft's Office app subscriptions which was launched only in November 2023, could add up to more than $10 billion in annualized revenue by 2026. Now, by being integrated into Microsoft Office applications like Word, Excel, and PowerPoint, 365 Copilot assists users in generating content, analyzing data, and enhancing productivity.

Noteworthily, it is Microsoft's integrated ecosystem with products like Office 365, Azure, and Windows which explains why the company is so profitable.

Product Integration Translates into Better Margins

In this case, once the software is developed, serving an additional customer incurs minimal incremental cost, as it can be distributed over the Internet without the need to allocate labor or physical computational resources for each new subscription as it is all automated. As such, SaaS (Software-as-a-Service) platforms are designed to scale easily, signifying that when the customer base grows, most of the expenses (like software customization and infrastructure) remain relatively fixed, and additional revenue translates directly into profits.

This partly explains why, despite AWS's (Amazon Web Services) much larger cloud computing platform with a 32% market share, it is the software giant that has a higher gross margin as shown below. Detailing further, Microsoft sells the Windows OS, productivity software, and business applications all bundled (or integrated) together, which means it uses cross-selling to generate sales. Thus, by leveraging existing customer relationships, it spends less marketing dollars, and it is more profitable.

Additionally, Microsoft’s net income margin being roughly four times higher than Amazon's also indicates it is far more efficient at converting revenue into profit, highlighting its strong operating model.

To this end, Microsoft’s AI business which mainly includes Copilot and Azure AI services (that help businesses integrate AI into their operations through analytics) has surpassed an annual revenue run rate of $13 billion at the end of December 2024, showing a remarkable growth of 175% YoY as shown below. The fact that operating income grew by 17% YoY while revenue only grew by 12% suggests that Microsoft’s product mix, which now includes a larger dose of AI is shifting toward more profitability.

Cautiously Optimistic Because of Risks

Now, when a company is deriving higher margins from its product suite, it is likely to sustain investment into product innovation, leading to more sustainable growth than Amazon for the AI infrastructure business. This is especially so if a worst-case scenario impacts the demand for GPU-as-a-service because of the distillation effect. Moreover, with a forward P/E around 14% ((31.09-36.14)/36.14) lower than that of Amazon as shown below, investors are currently paying less for each dollar of Microsoft’s future earnings.

This lower valuation, considered in light of its higher profitability, suggests that Microsoft may be undervalued relative to its earnings potential. Thus, as it continually embeds AI across its product line, both growth and profitability prospects should continue improving, thereby narrowing the 14% discount while pushing the stock higher. Thus, based on the discount applied to the share price of $410.5 at the time of writing, I have a target of $468.

Shifting to a cautionary note, from 9x two years back, the forward price-to-sales ratio has jumped to 11x, suggesting a sharp re-rating of the stock based on growth prospects, especially when compared with Amazon's P/S of 3.47x. However, Microsoft's 15% YoY revenue growth far outpacing the IT sector, by nearly 188% justifies its higher P/S. Still, this implies the software stock is likely to exhibit a higher degree of volatility in case of any slowdown in growth due to macroeconomic headwinds. Also, any competitive products likely to reduce its differentiation, for example Google announcing a breakthrough in the way its Gemini AI is integrated with its productivity suite called Workspace could put pressure on the stock, which means continued strong performance is critical.

Along the same lines, Microsoft's guidance for the third quarter fell short of what was expected by analysts on Wall Street, raising doubts about whether the initial momentum noted in AI revenue generation can be sustained, possibly in light of emerging players like DeepSeek. This may occur again as some customers may delay the launch of AI projects while assessing whether they can benefit from distillation.

Still, ending on a note of optimism, with its AI leadership position, Microsoft should continue to benefit as organizations transform their IT into super smart apps to deliver more automation. Also, its strategic partnership with OpenAI, and deep integration of AI across its product suite (Azure cloud services, Office 365 productivity tools, and GitHub developer platforms) positions it to capitalize more effectively on the AI revolution, not only by tapping into new revenue streams but also increasing profits thanks to its interconnected ecosystem which makes it harder for enterprise customers to switch to providers offering disparate solutions.

data.title: Disney: 2025 Deep-Dive Reveals A Wonderful Business And A Real Undervaluation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Walt Disney Company (NYSE:DIS) had a great Q1 2025 with the firm increasing profitability tangibly while continuing to grow revenues across their operations.

While the subscriber count fell 1% YoY, Disney appears to be positioning their DTC business to profit from multiple fronts which bolsters my expectations for the remainder of the fiscal year.

I updated my DCF valuation to reflect the improved cash flow generation at the company with the model suggesting a 26% undervaluation may be present in the stock.

In lieu of these fiscal and qualitative business improvements, I upgrade my rating for DIS from a Hold to a Buy.

Business Overview & Economic Moat

Disney is an entertainment icon. Since the firm’s founding back in 1923, the company has developed some of the most iconic franchises and characters while simultaneously monetising these assets incredibly effectively.

Disney’s current operations include their core TV and movie "Entertainment" business, theme-park and resort “Experiences” operations and a range of other multimedia enterprises.

Intellectual Property & Franchises

Moatiness arises for Disney primarily from the value of their powerful intangible assets such as Mickey Mouse, Toy Story, Cars, Winnie the Pooh and more modern additions like the Star Wars and Marvel franchises.

The enduring popularity of established franchises combined with Disney’s ability to create new ones drives their core TV, Movie and attractions businesses. Strong IPs increase the size and reliability of box office returns while providing multiple additional monetisation pathways which Disney can use to increase sales.

The company accordingly has large partnerships with clothing, toy and retail companies such as Hasbro (HAS), Mattel (MAT) and Primark, which allows Disney’s IPs to be translated into products ranging from Mickey Mouse pyjamas to Frozen-themed ski helmets and a staggering volume of toys.

If there is a physical product with space for print or branding, there is most likely a Disney version somewhere in the world.

What Disney has ultimately achieved is a phenomenal integration of their franchises into the everyday lives and psyches of people across the globe. Such a massive, entrenched and beloved presence is practically impossible to replicate and thus earns a wide economic moat.

Parks, Resorts and Cruise Line

Disneyland and Disney World theme parks are one of the best examples of how Disney extracts additional revenues by essentially allowing fans to engage with their beloved TV and movie characters in the physical world.

Disney Cruise Lines is another example of the same phenomenon, where customers can enjoy an all-inclusive cruise experience while in the company of their favorite on-screen heroes and friends.

While competitors certainly exist in the theme park, resort and cruise line industries, I believe these operations enjoy a wide economic moat thanks to their unique focus on bringing the characters and franchises to life. Simply put, a Disney holiday is not necessarily comparable to an otherwise similar offering from a competitor as these rival products are missing one key thing: they have no Disney fanfare in them.

Entertainment Business & DTC Streaming

Circling back to the core TV and Movie business, Disney historically made the majority of their revenues from what is now the declining linear TV business model. Since Netflix (NFLX) pioneered the DTC model, Disney has pivoted to a similar strategy with the launch of Disney+ in 2019.

While profitability is still short of what the lucrative linear model was able to achieve, in less than four years, Disney has produced operating profits from DTC which is impressive.

Disney also has a host of other media operations including Hulu, sports broadcaster ESPN, ABC and a 70% ownership stake in sports streaming company Fubo (which is the result of an agreement to combined Disney’s Hulu + Live TV business with Fubo).

ESPN has historically been a core component of Disney’s now declining linear networks business. Luckily for us, Disney is preparing the launch of an ESPN streaming service in 2025 which promises to combine sports streaming with betting and fantasy interactive elements.

This service will sit alongside Disney+ and be offered as a bundled service for Disney+ and Hulu customers. ESPN+ will also exist alongside Fubo which offers viewers access to the likes of ABC, ESPN, ESPN+, FOX, NBC and CBS without needing a cable TV subscription.

You would be forgiven if you thought I made a typo by including ESPN+ in the list of Fubo content. The Fubo service has indeed been confirmed to also include access to ESPN+.

As a result, I think that the ESPN+ service will be priced much lower (I expect around $12/month) than the approximate $80 per month Fubo charges.

I still think the overall transition to DTC is more the adoption of a new and more convenient format for television, rather than a revolutionary change within the entertainment industry.

Supporting my hypothesis is the integration of advertising into DTC streaming combined with gradual yet continuous price hikes, which ultimately are a necessity to ensure the profitability of these businesses.

With this in mind, I think Disney’s core DTC business finally has a wide economic moat which stems from the value of their IPs as well as a competitive position within the streaming industry. The Fubo deal has fortified this position, while the launch of the new ESPN service should add to this competitive advantage.

Fiscal Analysis – Q4 & FY2024

Disney is a profitable enterprise and, importantly, one that is improving rapidly.

Current gross, operating and net margins are around 36%, 14% and 7% respectively, which, while far cries from pre-Fox acquisition (2019) levels of 45%, 25%, and 22%, are markedly above pandemic-era lows of around 32%, 5% and 0% respectively.

The unfortunate combination that occurred when the additional debt and workload necessary to complete the 21st Century Fox deal combined with the sudden and drastic impact on earnings inflicted by the COVID-19 pandemic left Disney in a compromised state.

However, the recent return to solid growth combined with profitability in the streaming segment has reinvigorated earnings. Current ROA, ROE and ROIC of 3%, 6% and 5% are still depressed and only about half of those returns seen before 2020.

The degradation in ROIC came as a result of the capital acquired during the Fox deal. I expect this to improve towards the 10-14% range as Disney pays off additional debentures, continues to improve net earnings and executes on share buyback initiatives.

In order to buy shares, Disney will need cash, which, luckily the firm is generating at quite some rate. Using figures from the most recent Q1 earnings report, Disney’s FCF/Net income ratio is about 1.5x while their FCF per share is $4.67 compared to an EPS of just $3.07.

This ability to generate free cash flow above their accounted earnings illustrates how profitable the fundamental operations are at The Walt Disney Company. These metrics also compare starkly to rivals Netflix, who only has a FCF/net income ratio of 0.75x.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant shares the sentiment that Disney remains a fundamentally profitable enterprise by providing an “A+” profitability grade at present time. This falls in line with my hypothesis and is a great gut-check to perform at the start of the analysis process.

Analysis of Q1 2025 Earnings

Disney’s latest Q1 earnings report was very positive overall, with the firm exhibiting strong growth across their operational segments while producing a marked improvement in profitability.

Total revenues increased 5% YoY to $24.7 billion, with the results coming thanks to a 9% and 3% increase in entertainment and experiences revenues respectively. These results also came despite the continued impact on earnings produced by the divestiture of the Indian Disney+ Hotstar service.

Strong movie releases in the second half of 2024 including Moana 2, Mufasa: The Lion King and Inside Out 2 continued to buoy entertainment revenues in Q1 while elevated attendance figures at international parks helped boost experiences sales.

Disney’s also produced a massive increase in profitability, with the Entertainment segment in particular seeing a 95% increase in operating income to $1.7 billion.

Such robust results came thanks to the DTC business continuing to improve, with the service generating $293 million in operating income from $6 billion in revenues (segment operating margin of 5%) versus a $138 million loss in the same quarter last year.

Of course, linear networks still contributed a massive $1.1 billion in operating income (operating margin of 50%) despite only accounting for $2 billion in revenues.

Revenue per customer also increased an average of 4% across Disney’s DTC services, which is very positive to see. This decrease came despite a 1% total decrease in Disney+ subscribers.

While the drop in subscriber figures is a shame, strong performance in 2025 both theatrically and thanks to the launch of ESPN+ and partnering with Fubo should help grow Disney’s DTC business substantially.
